title: How AI Is Rewriting Human Nature | NOEMA
gist: A dense essay arguing that AI and computation are simultaneously driving a longevity/biohacking boom and a collapse in birthrates concentrated in secular, high-tech urban hubs, while transhumanism hardens into an elite infrastructure that diverges sharply between an American 'escape the body' model and a Chinese 'redesign the collective body' model.

insights.claim: AI's application to science — from mapping human proteins to optimizing sleep, diet and exercise — is turning meaningful longevity gains from aspiration into a plausible near-term goal, letting this generation 'biohack' its way to longer lives.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Signals a durable demand wave for longevity science, protein mapping, and consumer biohacking products among affluent, tech-adjacent consumers — an investable secular theme distinct from generic health/wellness.
insights.quote: But the promise of AI for science — from mapping human proteins to maximizing the benefits of sleep, diet and exercise — makes increases in longevity a plausible goal.
insights.locator: But the promise of AI for science

insights.claim: Biology may be the first domain where AI genuinely understands the world rather than merely predicting it, making the human body the first arena where AI matters in an existential sense.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Reframes where AI's first transformative economic payoff lands — bio/health rather than software productivity — worth weighting toward AI-for-biology plays over pure LLM productivity bets.
insights.quote: Biology may be the first domain where AI genuinely understands the world rather than merely predicting it.
insights.locator: I've come to believe that the first place

insights.claim: A craze for untested Chinese research peptides — analogous to the chemistry behind Ozempic — is spreading through Bay Area AI and biotech circles, shipped into the U.S. despite lacking FDA approval.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A gray-market supplement/peptide economy is forming ahead of regulation; both a demand signal for the next Ozempic-class compounds and a regulatory-arbitrage and safety risk to watch.
insights.quote: a craze for untested Chinese research chemicals, similar to the ones that became Ozempic, has been spreading
insights.locator: In mid-January, I visited San Francisco's Frontier Tower

insights.claim: Prominent wealthy Californians obsessed with their bodies, including Bryan Johnson opening a longevity clinic and hosting a 'Don't Die' summit, converged on Shanghai in late 2025 seeking stem cells and experimental treatments.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Cross-Pacific longevity tourism shows demand outrunning U.S. regulatory supply — an opening for offshore clinics and Chinese biotech to capture affluent Western longevity spend.
insights.quote: Bryan Johnson, the boy-king of immortality supplements, arrived to open a longevity clinic and to hold his “Don’t Die” summit
insights.locator: As 2025 came to a close

themes.theme: Fertility collapse concentrated in tech hubs

insights.claim: Birthrates are lowest and falling fastest in the secular, highly urbanized cities furthest ahead in advanced technology — Shanghai, San Francisco, New York, Seoul and Singapore all share extremely low birthrates.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Demographic decline clusters precisely in the highest-productivity, highest-cost innovation hubs — a structural headwind for domestic demand, labor supply and long-run asset values in exactly those markets.
insights.quote: Shanghai, San Francisco, New York, Seoul, Singapore — what these cities have in common is an extremely low birthrate.
insights.locator: Shanghai, San Francisco, New York, Seoul, Singapore

insights.claim: Silicon Valley's birthrate of 9.7 per 1,000 is significantly below the U.S. average and has dropped sharply; South Korea sits at roughly 5 per 1,000 (world's lowest) and China at a record-low 5.63 per 1,000.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Concrete magnitudes for pricing long-horizon demographic risk into East Asian and coastal-U.S. exposure; pension, housing and consumption models built on replacement-rate assumptions are mispriced.
insights.quote: In Silicon Valley, the birthrate of 9.7 births per 1,000 people is significantly lower than elsewhere in the U.S.
insights.locator: Around the world, birthrates in many tech hubs are in decline

insights.claim: A National Bureau of Economic Research study found early smartphone access in the U.S. contributed to lower birthrates, and countries where AI arrived faster (China, India) saw birthrates decline faster.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: If technology adoption causally suppresses fertility, tech penetration becomes a leading indicator for demographic decline in emerging markets, compressing their demographic-dividend windows.
insights.quote: A recent study from the National Bureau of Economic Research found that early smartphone access in the U.S. contributed to lower birthrates.
insights.locator: Research suggests this may be linked

insights.claim: In 2025 the global fertility rate reached what the U.N. called an 'unprecedented decline,' with the species appearing in aggregate to have chosen to stop reproducing at replacement levels, especially inside tech hubs.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Global, not just national, fertility deceleration removes the assumption that high-birthrate regions will backfill aging economies — a worldwide labor and consumption ceiling forming.
insights.quote: In 2025, the global fertility rate, calculating the number of births per woman of childbearing age, reached what the U.N. called an “unprecedented decline.”
insights.locator: Viewed in aggregate, however

insights.claim: Low birthrates are producing political instability across governance models — democratic systems that elevate the median voter are 'in big trouble' while hierarchical systems like China's face a base unwilling to stay at the bottom or reproduce.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Frames demographic decline as a driver of political-regime risk on both sides of the Pacific, a slow but systemic source of policy volatility for cross-border allocators.
insights.quote: Systems based on elevating the median man, like the American democratic order, are in big trouble.
insights.locator: And, as a consequence, a certain political tremor

insights.claim: Organized fertility withdrawal is emerging as political protest, from a Chinese feminist 'birth strike' against centralized power to Korea's 4B movement rejecting marriage, dating, childbirth and sex.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Fertility decline is partly a deliberate, ideological movement rather than a purely economic response, making it resistant to pro-natal subsidies and harder for states to reverse.
insights.quote: Korea has the 4B movement: no marriage, no dating, no childbirth, no sex.
insights.locator: Feminists in China talk of a birth strike

insights.claim: Even parents who do reproduce are increasingly restricting their children's access to screens, social media and AI, cultivating an artificial 'condition of beforeness' ahead of ubiquitous digital life.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A backlash among tech-literate elites against their own products foreshadows demand for screen-free schooling, analog childhood and digital-abstinence services — a contrarian consumer niche.
insights.quote: Many want their children to grow in an artificial condition of beforeness.
insights.locator: Even many of those who do have children

insights.claim: American transhumanism seeks to escape the body and treats the brain as a house for the soul, while Chinese transhumanism assumes the body will never leave us and therefore must be redesigned or fixed.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: This philosophical split predicts divergent investment: U.S. capital toward mind-uploading/BCI/consciousness, Chinese capital toward embodied biotech, gene editing and physical enhancement — different value chains to map.
insights.quote: If American transhumanism seeks to escape the body and considers the brain a sort of house for the soul, Chinese transhumanism assumes the body will never leave us and therefore must be redesigned or fixed.
insights.locator: The visions on either side of the Pacific are different

insights.claim: China's biotech push is state-led — spanning labs in Wuxi and startups in Pudong's Zhangjiang Science City — and treats the human body as an industrial input, comfortable with large-scale human experimentation as 'biopower is state power.'
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: State backing plus social tolerance for experimentation gives Chinese biotech a speed and scale advantage in trials that Western firms cannot match — a competitive edge in clinical throughput.
insights.quote: there is a sense that biopower is state power
insights.locator: Many of the Chinese scientists I brush shoulders with today

insights.claim: China's biotech ambition flows from a socialist-industrial legacy that treats the aging population as a mathematical equation to maximize, approaching the body 'as it once approached steel, cement and architectural blueprints.'
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Explains why Chinese biotech targets population-scale, industrial-style intervention (aging, robotics) rather than boutique individual enhancement — different addressable markets and policy tailwinds.
insights.quote: China is approaching the human body as it once approached steel, cement and architectural blueprints: as an ingredient in the next stage of development.
insights.locator: As the population rapidly ages

insights.claim: More than a quarter of China's population is expected to be over age 60 by 2040, intensifying the state's incentive to industrialize the body and biotech.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A hard demographic deadline that forces sustained state spending on eldercare, robotics and longevity biotech — a policy-driven demand floor for those sectors in China.
insights.quote: more than a quarter is expected to be over the age of 60 by 2040
insights.locator: As the population rapidly ages

insights.claim: Chinese and Chinese-adjacent populations are markedly more optimistic about AI and biotech than Americans, partly because they see their government as willing to discipline tech titans and pull malfunctioning products, whereas Americans suspect their state is in league with Big Tech.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Higher public trust can translate into faster domestic adoption of AI/biotech in China (e.g., self-driving, embodied AI) — an adoption-curve advantage that matters for revenue timing.
insights.quote: the Chinese general population and its elite intellectuals are more optimistic about AI and biotech than their American counterparts
insights.locator: In a general sense, it is striking

insights.claim: China's 'embodied AI' — models built inside humanoid robots and automobiles and trained on the human body itself — is developing 'shockingly quickly.'
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Points to embodied/humanoid robotics as China's distinctive AI frontier, aligned with its aging-labor problem — a sector where Chinese firms may lead over U.S. software-centric AI.
insights.quote: Chinese “embodied AI” systems, models built within humanoid robots or automobiles, are being developed shockingly quickly
insights.locator: At the same time, Chinese “embodied AI” systems

insights.claim: Advanced BCI experiments in China, led by figures like Ying Mao at Huashan Hospital, already let stroke, spinal-injury and Parkinson's patients play video games and better respond via wires attached to the brain.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Demonstrates China running clinical-stage BCI work in parallel with Western players like Neuralink — a second competitive front in the brain-computer interface race to track.
insights.locator: Back in Huashan Hospital in January

insights.claim: Transhumanism has stopped being a philosophy or subculture and become infrastructure and wealth, with personal health and reproduction turning into an elite technology in the rich world.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Frames longevity and enhancement as a stratifying luxury market with pricing power at the top end — capital concentrates where the wealthy buy biological advantage for themselves and heirs.
insights.quote: Transhumanism is no longer a philosophy, no longer a San Francisco subculture or vague futurism. It is infrastructure. It is wealth.
insights.locator: Transhumanism is no longer a philosophy

insights.claim: The wealthy and knowledgeable — exemplified by Elon Musk with more than a dozen children — are increasingly choosing to have many more children than traditional, while ordinary city-dwellers stop procreating, splitting reproduction along class lines.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Reproduction is bifurcating by wealth, foreshadowing a pronatalist elite consumer segment (fertility tech, genetic selection, private schooling) even as aggregate birthrates fall.
insights.quote: It seems the people with the most knowledge and money often choose to have many more children than is traditional; in the case of people like Elon Musk, more than a dozen.
insights.locator: Not everyone has decided to stop having children

insights.claim: Tech elites led by Musk and Bryan Johnson are explicitly building a future they and their heirs will 'reign over,' expecting their children to live in symbiosis with machine intelligences.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Signals concentrated elite capital deliberately steering AI, longevity and reproduction toward dynastic advantage — a driver of where frontier private funding actually flows.
insights.quote: As we give birth to AI, it’s possible that this is an emergency situation, that if we don’t take it seriously enough, we may fumble — and it’s not clear whether we humans have a future or not.
insights.locator: As such fears spread

insights.claim: Many urban non-parents appear to be calculating that children no longer function as 'embodied capital' — the return on raising a brood is poor in a future too uncertain to invest in.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Reframes low fertility as a rational ROI decision, implying pro-natal policy must change the economic return on children (cost, certainty) rather than merely exhort — informs which subsidies could work.
insights.quote: many city-dwellers have stopped procreating, perhaps sensing in some cases that, for them, the return on investment of a brood would be poor
insights.locator: Still, there is a sense that in the future

themes.theme: End of the individual and civilizational transition

insights.claim: The American middle-class configuration — sustained by cheap energy, an unrivaled defense force and manageable technology — is melting as AI, biotech and planetary-scale systems advance, dissolving the Enlightenment concept of the sovereign individual.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A macro thesis that the individual-consumer basis of Western economies is structurally eroding, with second-order implications for consumption-driven growth and political stability.
insights.quote: The individual — as a philosophical concept within the history of the Western Enlightenment — is ending, digested like a lump of food in stomach acid.
insights.locator: America built its civilization around the idea of the individual

insights.claim: The essay reads San Francisco's euphoric AI discourse amid visible urban decay as 'a civilization trying to outrun decay,' burning the candle at both ends.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A skeptical read on the AI boom's psychology — exuberance as denial of underlying societal decline — a sentiment cue for those watching AI-cycle froth.
insights.quote: the giddy, euphoric discourse of AI in a fairly run-down place had the vibe of a civilization trying to outrun decay
insights.locator: As I walked from Nob Hill to a dinner in the Tenderloin

insights.claim: Invoking Kojève, the author argues humans are reverting to animality — existing in idle contentment 'after the fashion of frogs and cicadas' without creating new history — as post-history sets in.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A philosophical 'end of history' frame for a post-work, AI-saturated society; useful lens for thinking about long-run demand for meaning, leisure and status goods.
insights.quote: humans will “perform musical concerts after the fashion of frogs and cicadas,” by which he meant they will exist in a state of idle contentment without creating new history
insights.locator: As French philosopher Alexandre Kojeve noted

insights.claim: Citing Freud's 'prosthetic God,' the author argues technology makes humans simultaneously more powerful and weaker, turning us into 'brains submerged in tanks' as we route even minor health questions to ChatGPT and DeepSeek.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: Points to deepening consumer dependence on AI for health triage — a real usage pattern underpinning demand for AI health assistants, alongside a dependency/de-skilling risk.
insights.quote: Technology makes us more powerful and weaker at the same time, Sigmund Freud wrote in his essay, “Civilization and Its Discontents,” about the concept of a prosthetic God.
insights.locator: Even today, many of us direct every minor health question

insights.claim: The author predicts that whatever major AI advances are coming will have 'happened' by the time his toddler sons reach elementary school, making cohabitation with an ever-present AI companion the essential skill of the next generation.
insights.speaker: Jacob Dreyer
insights.so_what: A compressed timeline for transformative AI (roughly this decade) framed around parenting — reinforces near-term urgency and demand for AI-native education and tooling.
insights.quote: by the time my toddler-age sons are in elementary school, whatever is going to happen will have happened
insights.locator: And yet, I believe that we are on the cusp
